data.title: First Solar: My Favorite Green Energy Stock, Climate-Change Fears Will Return
data.description:
First Solar, Inc. (NASDAQ:FSLR) has been pounded for price since June (10 months ago), initially from fears of a recession in utility demand as natural gas/coal prices sank (making solar less competitive for new projects), then from fears President Trump would end the lucrative government incentives for solar construction in the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022.

I would say both fears have some basis in reality. However, climate change and temperature gains on the planet are not slowing down (if anything accelerating). Eventually, investor demand for stocks like First Solar will return as the desirability of low emission/pollution energy generation continues to grow.

Solar (photovoltaic - PV) demand has largely been a function of sliding technology costs, government incentives, and competing/alternative energy inputs for electricity production.

Another positive in the future is I fully expect oil/gas prices to eventually spike. This could happen sooner than many think possible, if President Trump attacks Iran or Russian aggression in Eastern Europe expands from Ukraine to neighboring nations. In terms of long-term supply, at today's sub-US$65 crude oil price, for example, U.S. production levels will likely decline, and Middle Eastern nations will find it difficult to pay their bills, over a period of several years. Remember, including the effects of general inflation - $62 crude oil in April 2025 is the same as $50 in 2019 or the level of 25 years ago in 2000. So, I am confident an important fossil fuel price bottom is approaching.

My view is we are getting close to a washout point for Wall Street expectations around solar energy names regarding U.S. government tax/credit help for the industry and bottom-of-the-barrel costs for gas/oil/coal. Then, contrarian thinking will be a paramount positive to contemplate, as price discovery hits a bottom.

Believe it or not, the cost for alternative natural gas supplies have roughly doubled over the past year in America. If current hopes for different types of nuclear-based electric production prove illusive (especially for AI datacenter demand), and dirty energy options like coal continue to be frowned upon, clean energy solutions like wind and solar should remain in an elevated demand position.

But that's not all the bad news for First Solar share pricing this year. The latest 2025 bust in the U.S. stock market, as investor fears of recession bump higher (on Trump tariff announcements), has only piled on to the negative sentiment train for solar names.

To me, First Solar is suffering from so many negatives in April, taking the other side may be the smartest decision. We could be reaching for a multi-year cyclical price low in the solar sector, while the FSLR operating business is still hitting on all cylinders. Consequently, I am keeping my Buy rating from last year, and looking to add shares in my personal account.

Bullish Business Forecast

Actual operations are doing better than fine in early 2025, and management guidance remains solidly positive. (Of course, this was before the tariff mess, trade war began last week.)

The company achieved record sales of $4.2 billion and EPS of $12.02. New thin-film PV panel production facilities in the U.S. are being built with cash flow coming in the door each day, helped by IRA credits.

Utility-scale solar buildout is all but a requirement (in my mind at least) to match the projected rise in AI datacenter demand over the immediate future.

2025 management guidance is very bullish, with analysts projecting +31% sales and +58% EPS growth this year.

Cheap Valuation on Cloudy Sentiment

Before the trade war announcement last week, First Solar was sitting on one of the top 3-year growth rate estimates for any green energy name. In fact, forward 2027 EPS projections work out to an ultra-low 4.1x earnings at today's $128 stock price. Why invest in Big Tech high-flyers at 25x to 100x EPS in two years, when unloved FSLR is going for just 4x?

The forward 1-year P/E estimate of 4.7x is by far the lowest of any larger-cap green energy pick, outside of businesses operating in China. Granted, the vast majority of clean energy names (of any size) are not projected to earn much money in 2025, with slight income projected for 2026.

Another huge plus for me is First Solar operates at the highest final net-profit margin on sales of any important green energy business today. A function of very little debt and high cash levels (generating interest income), plus an established business run conservatively, FSLR should be your first choice in solar (from a risk-adjusted investment standpoint).

On trailing fundamental metrics, the latest 10.7x P/E ratio and 11.4x accounting cash flow number are well below average for this stock trading since 2006. In terms of valuation range, they are closer to the 20th and 25th percentile, respectively.

In my research, when nobody wants to own First Solar at a cyclical low, and earnings are sketchy (the opposite of today), shares have traded closer to tangible book value. The current 1.75x ratio is sitting quite a distance below its median yearly average of 3x. I believe this stat is quite reasonable if EPS continues to grow rapidly.

When we review changing cash and debt positioning over the decades, First Solar's enterprise valuation is equally compelling. EV to EBITDA of 4.9x and sales of 2.3x on a "forward" 2025 basis are incredibly inexpensive. On EBITDA, FSLR is trading in the 10th percentile vs. its 19-year history, while sales are roughly the 40th percentile.

Seeking Alpha's computer-sorting system ranks First Solar as one of the more attractive stocks on Wall Street using its Valuation Grade tool. Today's overall "A+" score is hard to ignore as a long-term investor. Of course, the continuation of government tax incentives boosting solar farm construction in America (and IRA credits helping FSLR specifically) will be a prerequisite for the valuation story to remain intact.

Final Thoughts

To me, the biggest variable operating issue and investment risk for First Solar is how Trump and the Republicans handle incentives for utility-scale solar buildout in America. The company's U.S. manufacturing focus is a real plus for domestic sales, if tariffs are raised on Chinese solar panel producers in particular.

Talk has been Trump would like to replace the direct tax/credit payments to buyers of solar projects, and replace them with high tariffs on foreign supplies. This would definitely cause a hiccup in U.S. demand a year or two down the road for this company.

Yet, overseas nations could retaliate by taxing/tariffing First Solar projects using American-made panels and parts in their locations. Such an effort could kill future projects and cause some disarray in terms of who pays the new penalties, as current projects under construction have been signed and contracted all over the world. Would the customer or First Solar be responsible, when this extra cost was not written into a previously signed deal?

First Solar does have a growing India manufacturing and sales presence, which may be less affected by the U.S. trade war with the world. However, I would categorize the U.S. tariff policies announced last week as a net negative for the business, until more clarity on Trump's final tariff rates and foreign retaliation become clear. The company was able to perform/compete fine on its own when lower government incentives and tariff rates were the norm many years ago. For sure, FSLR is the leading solar panel maker in the world today, outside of Chinese influence.

In the end, in my view, worsening climate change problems on the globe, paired with great First Solar PV technologies, expanding production and sales outside of China, alongside an existing low valuation, should be purchased during a stock market panic situation. There are too many tailwinds at your back to sell or walk away from this investment idea. I would rather own an A+ valuation vs. an "F" score, all other variables being equal, if we are headed into recession.

I rate First Solar a Buy and hold a growing position size. For a 12 to 18-month outlook, potential upside back to $300 a share for 2024's high trade (best-case scenario), vs. downside risk to $60 and tangible book value (worst-case scenario), are the gain/loss parameters I am contemplating from the current $128 share quote. My opinion is the lower FSLR drops from this number, the more interesting it gets for long-term holders.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: Whirlpool: Appliances Winner On Tariffs And Housing Market Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Whirlpool (NYSE:WHR) is a buy on consistent dividend payments for over 70 years and on trading at decade lows. If the company can manage its debt, it can offer a 15% return on investment over the next 5-10 years, driven by positive housing market developments, and a 7% dividend yield. All else being equal, it stands to benefit from increased tariffs in the short term because of its large US manufacturing capability.

Short business description

Whirlpool Corporation is a leading global manufacturer and marketer of home appliance products, with sales roughly equally distributed between dishwashing (18%), cooking (24%), laundry (27%) and refrigeration (31%). The company’s history spans over a century, having been founded in 1911 and going public in 1955. It derives 62% of its revenue from North America, 21% from Latin America and the remaining portion from its international businesses. The Whirlpool brand is a household name, well-known across the globe and trusted by consumers.

The stock price performance highlights immense volatility sustained throughout the 57 years covered in the chart above, reflective of its reliance on sporadic customer demand and leveraged structure. The housing crisis in 2009 and then the $820M interest expense in FY 2024 are a testament to that fact. Nevertheless, despite the volatility, the long-term trend is strongly positive. Shareholder returns are further boosted by the generous capital allocation policy, the company boasting a 70-year track record of sustained or increasing dividends.

Tariffs - Short-Term Benefits

The implications of tariffs on Whirlpool are a mixed story. In 2018, washing machines were a direct target of tariff programs, following a petition filed by Whirlpool itself in response to South Korean imports flooding the market and hurting local production. Whirlpool is the “last major US-based appliance maker” and manufactures 80% of products sold in the U.S. locally. The action by the trade commission to impose tariffs of up to 50% on imported washing machines prompted Samsung and LG to open manufacturing hubs in the United States, as per the Trade Commission’s policy continuation report from 2020. Unfortunately, key figures from the report that detail the impact of tariffs on final prices are redacted, and other sources seem to offer mixed information. Below, I quote the high-level overview provided in the report:

Before the implementation of the safeguard measure (January 2017-December 2017), prices for imported LRWs [large residential washers] were below those for U.S.-produced LRWs in 26 of 29 comparisons (*** units). During the January 2018-June 2020 period, prices for imported product were below U.S.-produced LRWs in 49 of 70 comparisons (*** units)

We can tell from this that tariffs i) made American products somewhat more competitive, from ~10% more competitive samples (3 out of 29 total comparison samples) before the measures were implemented, to ~30% more competitive samples after implementation (21 out of 70 total comparison samples) and ii) it prompted manufacturing to move to the United States. We cannot directly determine how much more competitive to quantify the impact on Whirlpool’s bottom line.

Nevertheless, due to the magnitude and breadth of current tariffs, I expect short-term benefits for Whirlpool as competition scrambles to increase production in the US.

New & Existing Housing - Key Sales Drivers

All else being equal, Whirlpool is a price taker, much like any commodity producer. The relatively poor pricing power of the company derives from the fact that sales of new appliances vary in line with broad economic indicators reflecting homeownership, which is when the bulk of demand for essential appliances arises. Management elucidates this fact in its latest investor presentation, citing 15% of total industry demand coming from new construction, and 20% of total industry demand classified as discretionary, which means purchases of existing homes.

Of course, all else being equal is a strong statement here. We can only assume that business performance depends on changes in macroeconomic variables for as long as the company upholds the same quality standard of design, manufacturing and culture of innovation as it has done in the past. This is because the industry is highly competitive, and failing to adapt to customer demand may be rapidly punished.

The two demand drivers I’ve mentioned are captured by macroeconomic data. Demand from new construction is reflected directly in the New Privately-Owned Housing Units Started. Discretionary demand is a function of homeowner affordability, which is reflected by economic variables such as prevailing long-term mortgage rates and inflation. Using this data, we can test the idea that Whirlpool is a cyclical company, with its profits dependent on housing market dynamics. The economic data is available through FRED and the financial data through value-quant.uk.

In the following table, I show the correlation between an annual change of the rolling 4-quarter sum of gross profits and several lagged and differentiated versions of the change in the 30-year fixed mortgage rate, new housing starts, consumer sentiment and disposable income. All data series have been logged first, to stabilize the variance, reduce the effect of outliers and make it more suitable for correlation analysis. Gross profit was chosen as the earnings metric to reflect profitability after production costs such as material costs and wages, which is critical for a manufacturing company. The rolling sum over the past 4 quarters smooths out quarterly changes, while the annual change captures dynamic growth trends that respond to changes in macroeconomic conditions and makes analysis more robust by removing the trend in the data. Another way to think about it is that the stock price reacts to changes in profitability, not necessarily the level. If a large company reduces earnings by a large percentage, it is likely that the stock performance would be affected even though the company still generates high earnings, in absolute terms.

YoY Δ Rolling 4Q Gross Profit Correlation
Δ newhomes_9 0.29
Δ newhomes_12 0.4
Δ newhomes_18 0.46
Δ newhomes_24 0.41
Δ mort30_24 -0.22
Δ sentim_12 0.25
Δ sentim_18 0.36
Δ sentim_24 0.37
Δ cpi_18 -0.31
Δ cpi_24 -0.39

Note that, for example, Δ newhomes_9 refers to the difference between the current month and the value of the New Housing Starts data series 9 months ago.

The results show a moderate-to-strong positive correlation with new housing starts (0.29 to 0.46) and consumer sentiment (0.25 to 0.37) over 9-24-month lagged differences. They show a moderate-to-strong negative correlation of -0.22 with the 30-year fixed mortgage rate over a 24-month lag and with the CPI over the 18- and 24- month lags of -0.31 and -0.39, respectively. The results corroborate management’s emphasis on new construction and discretionary spending as drivers of demand for Whirlpool’s products, where consumer sentiment may be a better proxy variable for the latter due to credit-based purchasing behavior. The negative relationship of changes in the consumer price index and in mortgage rates supports the intuition that pressures on affordability hinder home-moving and thus demand for home appliances.

With this analysis, I highlight the factors that matter for Whirlpool's profitability, but I cannot predict how they will develop in the future. It is likely that a rapid recovery in the housing market is not on the horizon, and as a result, the business could suffer further, even despite any added benefit from factors such as tariffs.

Risks

The company has extended significant debt offerings at rates higher than what they previously used to obtain, as summarized in the following chart taken from the latest 10-K filing. The company’s interest expense rose to $358m in FY 2024, up from $351M in FY 2023 and just $190M in FY2022.

The company’s strong brand portfolio provides support, enabling further borrowing or refinancing, particularly for the $1.85 billion due in 2025, which management expects “to repay through a combination of refinancing, cash flow generation and cash on hand.” Furthermore, they are divesting of certain global assets anticipating a “less global world”, which would be a boost to liquidity: “On February 20, 2024, Whirlpool's wholly owned subsidiary, Whirlpool Mauritius Limited, executed the sale of 30.4 million equity shares of Whirlpool India via an on-market trade. The transaction reduced Whirlpool's ownership in Whirlpool India from 75% to 51%, and generated sales proceeds of approximately $462 million on settlement.”

The second important risk factor that I see is the “all else being equal” which I alluded to before in the article. We can rely on macroeconomic variables to gauge business performance only insofar all other key factors remain constant, which is to say that Whirlpool continues to uphold the same execution standard it has done over the decades with respect to its competition. To cite from the latest annual report: "Competition in the major home appliance industry is intense, including competitors such as BSH (Bosch), Electrolux, Haier, Hisense, LG, Mabe, Midea, Panasonic and Samsung, many of which are increasingly expanding beyond their existing manufacturing footprint."

Valuation

The valuation is driven by the regression to the mean idea - discretionary spend will recover, and new housing starts should improve to address undersupply. Management is selling the same story, saying in its March 3rd 2025 presentation to "Expect discretionary demand recovery to improve driven by existing home sales in near to mid term" and estimating a "U.S. housing undersupply of 3 to 4 million units".

If we look at the stock price, it seems to follow a step-wise ascent with volatility around the trend, which I've summarized in the following figure.

The current stock price is a -40% deviation from the implied long-term trend.

For a cash flow valuation, I am estimating the normal free cash flow for Whirlpool at $500M per year, although they made notably more in FY20 and FY21. I have chosen to discount those years as outliers for a more conservative figure, and rely instead on a rough average for the 2015-2019 period. The figure I get is $621M, which I have revised downward to reflect the possibly persistent lower discretionary spend caused by the negative macroeconomic environment over the next few years.

I will use the exit multiple valuation method, and estimate growth for the next 10 years at 3% a year, and an exit multiple of 11, numbers which are in line with historical free cash flow growth and the recent market FCF multiple post 2018.

Parameters Assumptions
Growth Period (years) 10
Growth Rate 3%
Discount rate (IRR) 15%
Exit multiple 11
Starting FCF ($M) 500

The results show the company to be fairly valued at the current price for a 15% return, out of which the dividend yield based on average dividend payout of $360M per year is roughly 7%.

Conclusion

I've hinted at the potential benefit of tariffs on Whirlpool based on the effects of the last set of tariffs enacted in 2017, which the company petitioned for itself, and I have fleshed out using economic data management's idea that Whirlpool's success depends on positive developments in new construction and consumer discretionary spend. I believe the current price offers a good entry point for a 15% return on investment based on historical stock price levels and a conservative free cash flow valuation. The thesis is not without its caveats, as there is a strong assumption of Whirlpool maintaining its competitive position in a fierce, commodity industry. Furthermore, negative developments in the housing market and macro environment could depress profits further, which could lead to dividend cuts or liquidity problems. This is not an investment where I see, with a high degree of confidence, a 0 percent probability of permanent loss of capital.

You can check out my two other articles here on Seeking Alpha for ideas with - in my opinion - much more limited downside.

data.title: Indian Financial Markets - An Inflection Point In Their Global Role?
data.description:
By Indrani De, CFA, PRM, Head of Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell | Robin Marshall, Director, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell | Belle Chang, Senior Manager, Global Investment Research, FTSE Russell

In recent years, Indian financial markets have grown sharply and attracted strong foreign portfolio inflows into both equity and fixed income markets, helped by strong economic growth and relatively stable inflation. This has driven strong performance in both asset classes, relative to EM peers and G7 markets.

In recent years, Indian capital markets have grown sharply, and attracted significant foreign investor inflows, helped by macro policy continuity, less restrictions on capital flows, a relatively stable exchange rate, and favourable growth and inflation outcomes. We note that the Indian government bond market is now the 4th largest in APAC, and the equity market the 4th largest in the FTSE Asia Pacific Index. Unlike previous Fed tightening cycles, Indian government bond yields did not increase sharply in 2022-23, and have fallen since, and the Indian equity market prospered in this period.

In this Part 2 of our India financial market series[1], we look at key capital market drivers in recent years, and the impact of eligible Indian government bonds joining FTSE government bond indices from September 2025. We also look at the apparent paradox of reduced Indian FDI and equity portfolio investment inflows, alongside stable bond market inflows.

Macro back-drop - India’s balanced growth since Covid

India has experienced balanced growth in the bounce-back from Covid, driven both by buoyant infrastructure and capex, and resilient consumer spending, and has been one of the fastest growing APAC and EM countries. In contrast to China and Brazil, where growth has trended lower, as the demographic dividend faded, India has shown faster real GDP growth in the 5-7% region since Covid (Chart 1).

Chart 1: Selected Country Real GDP Growth (Quarterly, YOY)

In addition, India has shown more stable inflation in recent years (Chart 2) and did not experience as big an inflation shock as Brazil, or the deflationary strains seen in China. Inflation fell to 3.6% yoy, below the 4% mid-point of the RBI’s 2-6% target in February, vindicating the decision to ease policy in February.

Chart 2: Selected Country Inflation Rates (Monthly, YOY)

Successful inflation targeting enhanced RBI policy credibility

The RBI adopted its 2-6% inflation target in 2016 and sought to stabilise inflation expectations and build credibility for the target, by not reducing rates too quickly. Brazil’s adverse experience of needing to reverse 2024 rate cuts after an inflation rebound is worth noting in that regard. With inflation returning to the mid-point of the target range, the RBI made its first rate cut since 2020 at the February 2025 meeting and pivoted to focus more on growth. Chart 3 shows Indian 10yr and 2yr yields falling in advance of the first RBI rate cut, as investors locked in higher yields to reduce re-investment risk.

Chart 3: Indian Policy Rate And Government Bond Yields

Foreign access to Indian capital markets enhanced

The investability of India’s fixed income markets has been enhanced by the local currency Indian Government Bond market (IGB) tripling in size since 2014, becoming the fourth-largest government bond market within the APAC region, following Japan, China, and South Korea.

Foreign bond inflows grew in recent years (Chart 4), helped by high Indian yields and ongoing global bond index inclusion. Eligible local currency, fixed-rate IGBs will enter the FTSE EMGBI (EM Government Bond Index), AGBI (Asian Government Bond Index) and APGBI (Asia Pacific Government Bond Index) indices from September 2025. As estimated in September 2024, India will likely account for 9.35% of the FTSE EMGBI, following China’s 57.85%; and 9.73% of FTSE AGBI, following China’s 60.21% and Korea’s 14.12%. Indeed, Chart 4 shows foreign net purchase of INR 1.2tr since end-2023.

Chart 4: India Foreign Bond Portfolio Investment Inflows (INR BN)

Moreover, the RBI’s efforts to offer more FX hedging tools, loosen restrictions for foreign investors, and keep the INR relatively stable vs USD have all allowed easier access to foreign investors across asset classes. Easier market access, the balanced fundamental growth outlook and rising capex were major drivers for strong portfolio investment inflows into the Indian equity markets (Chart 5), as we discussed in Part 1 of this series.[2]

Chart 5: India Foreign Equity Portfolio Investment Flows (INR BN)

Government bonds also helped by fiscal debt target

On fiscal policy, the Indian government continues to manage the trade-offs between growth and deficit reduction. The government has implemented a 50% central government debt/GDP target by 2031 (about 7% below current levels), designed to address the twin deficit issues on current account and fiscal policy, which often disrupted policymaking. The fiscal deficit is forecast to decline to 4.4% of GDP in FY2025-26. India’s general government debt/GDP ratio of 83% compares well with the G7 economies, and is now below China’s, after being almost twice as high in 2009 (Chart 6). With nominal GDP growing at close to 10%, India also has scope to finance further infrastructure development by issuing debt, without causing the debt/GDP ratio to grow rapidly.

Chart 6: General Government Debt/GDP Ratio

Despite yields falling slowly, IGBs outperformed

Aided by a stable currency, the FTSE India Government Bond Index outperformed Asian peers in USD terms in 2023 and 2024. The FTSE India Government Bond Index saw 7.5% and 6.9% total returns vs FTSE AGBI’s 4.1% and 2.3% respectively in 2023 and 2024. Expectations for further global bond index inclusion and the start of the RBI’s rate cutting cycle, have placed further downward pressure on IGB yields, and the proposed reduced budget deficit would ease supply pressure.

Chart 7: Indian Government Bond Performance Versus Peers (Total Return, USD)

But equity markets witnessed foreign outflows since 2024; why?

However, Indian equities suffered net foreign outflows since H2, 2024 (Charts 8) with expensive valuation being a major driver. A portfolio substitution effect may also have driven foreign investors from Indian into Chinese equities, lifted by the substantial monetary and fiscal stimulus announced in September 2024, optimism around a Chinese AI breakthrough in February 2025, and a cheap valuation. In contrast, foreign inflows into fixed income in 2024-25 have been stable, likely reflecting the attractive level of IGB yields versus other markets.

Chart 8: Foreign Portfolio Flows Into Indian Bonds And Equities Since 2015 (INR BN)

FDI net inflows also fell post-Covid…

Turning to FDI flows, cross-border FDI flows have slowed globally since the initial recovery post-Covid, and have fallen in India since 2022, even if they rose in the years before Covid as Chart 9 shows (mainly from Singapore and the US, and into the manufacturing sector and information and communication industries). Also note that after Covid struck, in 2020-21, Indian pharmaceutical industries saw strong FDI flows for vaccine production, helping fuel capex and economic growth.

Chart 9: India's Foreign Direct Investment Flows (Quarterly, USD MN)

…and FDI inflows into India have seldom been large, as a share of GDP

Globally, net FDI inflows fell back sharply from 2022 onwards, after overlapping shocks from Ukraine and Covid-led inflation, and a sharp rise in G7 interest rates. But we note India has never been the beneficiary of high FDI inflows, as a share of GDP, unlike China until recently (Chart 10). Indeed, it could be said that India has achieved strong economic growth despite not attracting high FDI inflows, rather than because of it. Globally, net FDI inflows have fallen since the high point of globalization and multilateralism just before the GFC, in the early 2000s. But the Indian government has put in place a new policy framework to attract increased FDI, including lifting the ceiling on FDI in the insurance sector from 74% to 100% (February 2025).

Chart 10: FDI Inflows Into India And China (% Of GDP)

Indian GDP is also domestic demand driven, as shown in the composition of GDP. As Chart 11 shows, India, compared to its regional peers, is less dependent on external and more on domestic demand. Capex is important to fundamental growth, but consumer spending is the greatest driver for India’s GDP.

Chart 11: GDP Breakdown (2023, % OF GDP)

In conclusion...

Growth in Indian capital markets reflects a credible policy regime, improved foreign access, and investability via global bond indices. Strong and domestically-driven growth shields India from global uncertainty.

India’s capital markets are growing rapidly and attracting strong portfolio inflows. Fixed income has outperformed peers since before Covid, boosted by a relatively stable currency and higher yields. Equities also outperformed peers, helped by strong capex and consumption growth. Recent foreign equity outflows were mainly due to expensive valuations and a foreign investors’ portfolio substitution effect.

FDI flows have slowed, but the impact on Indian growth could be rather limited as FDI is not a big growth driver. The domestic focus of the Indian economy sets it apart from many of its EM peers, and is potentially a strength in a global economy facing trade shocks and fragmentation.

IGB yields, unlike previous Fed tightening cycles, did not increase in 2022-23, reflecting greater maturity, investability and stronger market fundamental[3]. India’s FTSE bond index inclusion from September 2025 may boost foreign investor inflows further. Easier monetary policies could also help support IGB returns. More progress in fiscal deficit reduction and towards the 50% debt/GDP target would bode well for a ratings upgrade.

Footnotes

[1] Part 1 of the India financial markets series see India: A structural growth story. But have valuations run ahead?, Belle Chang and Indrani De, FTSE Russell, January 2025.

[2] See "Does the twin deficit hypothesis exist in India? Empirical evidence from an asymmetric non-linear cointegration approach", Mallick, Behera and Murthy, Journal of Economic Asymmetries, November 2021.

[3] See "The EM crisis that never was", Robin Marshall, FTSE Russell, June 2023.

data.title: SABA: Why This High-Yield Fund Is A Buy Amid Market Chaos
data.description:
In this market, I love the Saba Capital Income & Opportunities Fund II (NYSE:SABA). It is managed by Saba Capital, under credit expert and closed-end fund (CEF) activist Boaz Weinstein. Weinstein is famous for taking down the London whale, and the Covid crisis lead to his best year. By May, he was up some 70%+. Most people were not.

This isn’t a run-of-the-mill fund that’s investing in some kind of Morningstar asset classification. Looking at the SABA portfolios one of the things I believe the firm does best is put together a very well diversified collection of bets. There’s credit, private credit, SPACs, reinsurance, and closed-end funds (with additional value being created through activism). The names in the portfolio also show this is a creative fund manager looking everywhere for opportunity; Grayscale Ethereum Classic Trust (OTC:ETCG), Cannae Holdings (CNNE), Capri Holdings (CPRI), S&P 500 (SPY) puts and China Evergrande Group (OTC:EGRNF). Here are the key asset allocations:

Private Fund 17.75%
Closed End Funds (Globally) 13.42%
Corporate Bonds 10.26%
Common Stock 9.81%
Senior Loans 8.49%
Money Market Funds 5.71%
Investment Trusts 4.70%
Unit Trust 3.93%
Credit Default Swap 2.30%
Options 1.92%

The private fund is for about 50% invested into a cat-bond fund.

Here are the top-10 investments:

Stone Ridge Opportunities Fund LLP 10.98
Bitwise 10 Crypto Index Fund 4.39
ALTERNATIVE CAPITAL INVETMTS FUND III LP 2 4.01
Grayscale Ethereum Classic Trust 3.83
MARKIT CDX HY S43 5Y 2/29 ICE 2.36
Revlon Intermediate Holdings IV LLC, Term Loan 2.30
Platinum Asia Investments, Ltd. 2.22
RR Donnelley & Sons Co. 2.01
Cannae Holdings, Inc. 1.98
Level 3 Financing, Inc. 1.92

The primary reason to buy SABA in the middle of this crisis is that its discount to net asset value has widened to 12%+. That’s from 9.72% before all the tariff chaos. A very close peer, BRW, trades at a 10% discount after trading at 7.25% before the tariff chaos. BRW utilizes 20% leverage where SABA is unlevered. According to CEFA data the average CEF trades at a 7.1% discount right now. Equity CEFs trade at an average discount of 7% and fixed income funds at a discount of 7.18%. Relatively speaking I’d say fixed income CEFs look quite attractive.

SABA has often traded only about 1% wider than BRW. The current gap of roughly 2%+ suggests a relative mispricing. The SABA fund is also a newer fund that seems like it isn't fully invested yet. SABA Capital acquired both funds through activism replacing ineffective management. BRW was taken over slightly earlier.

A key criticism has, of course, been the perennial discount the funds traded at. Under Weinstein, the discounts narrowed until being blown back out during this crisis. It’s possible we haven’t seen the bottom yet, but I fully expect this fund will ultimately get its discount narrowed again.

The fund's annualized distribution rate, based on the absolute Feb rate, of 7.68% is paid monthly. That’s quite a bit below BRW's 12%+. I believe SABA will eventually get to that level as well. SABA also charges lower gross fees (3.21%) compared to BRW (5.72%). Both include their interest costs which brings the number up so high. The net fees are closer at 1.86% vs. 1.54%. It wouldn't surprise me if leverage is introduced at SABA as the portfolio gets built out.

Saba Capital is famous for going after fund behemoths like BlackRock (BLK) to improve shareholder value and governance in the CEFs they own. This means Saba needs to manage the discounts on its funds as well. Otherwise, the activist would get ridiculed and blown off a lot more often. I expect, over time, SABA and BRW will increasingly look alike. Right now around 50% of the BRW portfolio names are also present in the SABA notes. I wouldn’t be surprised at all if the funds are merged down the road.

To wrap things up, I think the SABA Fund isn’t especially vulnerable to the trade war that started early April. The fund mostly invests in credit which is holding up better than equities. The credit expertise also comes in handy to hedge the portfolio.

The current discount to NAV of 12%+ is a solid opportunity, given this is now a well-managed CEF. The fund sports a 7%+ distribution yield. The portfolio is interesting. Management is well incentivized to close the discount to NAV again. These are all ingredients I like in CEFs. Keep in mind that if the U.S. tariffs remain, the fund is likely to follow the market down, although it owns some protective puts, and the discount could potentially widen out further. However, I've almost doubled my position during this sell-off. Weinstein commented on the sell-off that it's a sell-the-dip opportunity. When the market ultimately normalizes some, I'd expect the discount to narrow quickly.

data.title: Bilibili: Excellent Value With Persistent Cost Cutting Efforts, But Need To Wait For A Better Entrance
data.description:
Thesis

Bilibili (NASDAQ:BILI) (OTCPK:BLBLF) presents a buying opportunity as cost-cutting efforts have been persistent since 2022, leading to a likely catalyst of earnings outperformance, moving away from the negative territory starting from 2025. Management guidance had expressed ambition to increase its margin through the continued expansion of advertising and gaming revenue. In addition, this is a company with cash of $2.3B vs. c$7B in market capitalization to support liquidity and any further buyback programs. My current target price range is at $22-30. I remain optimistic about its earnings but cautious about the current US equities market environment with elevated volatilities that will remain for a while as global trading partners retaliate with Trump's policies. On top of my "buy" rating, I suggest a wait-and-see approach for investors to limit the global macro exposures that cannot be diversified away. Right now is not the best entry point.

Company overview and recent performance

The root of the company is its platform for the ACG (animation, comics, and games) genre in China, arguably inspired by Japan's websites such as Nico Nico Douga. The content of the platform is primarily from a combination of creators and licensed anime. It earns its revenue through value-added services such as subscriptions, onsite advertising, and mobile games where it collaborates with other game developers.

The company was founded in 2009 and went public on NASDAQ in 2018. By the time of IPO, it had already gained 93 million monthly active users (MAU). In 2023 and 2024, it had reached 330 million and 341 million. Growth through users and revenue had already dimmed. The key driver of performance is margin expansion as the company increases its advertising and mobile gaming's share of total earnings.

Revenue breakdown by activity

Growth in advertising is primarily driven by "improved advertising product offerings and enhanced advertising efficiency". Gaming revenue in 2024 had also seen a significant rise with the launch of Sau Guo: Mou Ding Tian Xia. Management remains optimistic about those profit channels reflected through a long-term gross margin target range of 40-45% vs. 64% in 4q24. With that said, gross margin has been on an improving trend since 2023 (see q4 presentation pg. 5) as the company made it a priority amid elevated employee layoffs.

User growth has slowed but not profits

As an ACG-focused content platform based in China, I'd argue there are only so many customers that you can attract. At the same time, I'd also say that there is an element of elasticity from the subscribers that contributes to the VAS bottom line given their ACG consumption preferences. With a platform that is constantly improving its infrastructure, platform synergy, user experience, and profit generation from the mentioned channels, earnings growth will likely persist. The unmatching growth expectations for revenue and net income highlight that the company is focusing more on getting "better"/efficient and not bigger. The consistent improvement in advertising revenue is a testament to that as it began to attract more quality brand partnerships (see 4q24 presentation).

Corporate actions

Lastly, considering some of the latest corporate actions, Bilibili had announced a $200 million ADS share buyback program in 2024 where it had spent $16.4 million as of December. The program lasts for 24 months.

Fundamentals and valuation

The trend for financials is overall positive, with strong 4q24 earnings and guidance, and also expectations from the sell-side. Meanwhile, management views the year as "pivotal" with evidence for robust advertising and gaming revenue generation.

Cost items to revenue

As previously discussed, gross margin improvements have been substantial, with an ambitious target of 40-45% range in the long run. In my view, if that level were to be reached, then future earnings would likely beat the current consensus substantially given that expectations now can be sustained by a gross margin level of 50-60%. As for how likely the gross margin increase is, it is quite reasonable and likely. Key cost items are revenue-sharing costs, content, servers, and other intellectual property. These have limited variability against advertising.

In addition, note that we are assuming that common shares outstanding remain constant at 416 million and not considering the potential market reaction on future buybacks. These can provide additional upside.

See implications of the current consensus EPS...

...And also how those expectations translate to price upside using residual income valuation

In my valuation, I used the implied equity risk premium from NYU professor Aswath Damodaran's observations and a beta value of 0.95. I used the residual income valuation model to limit the sensitivity of growth and discount rates for a company that is still generating negative cash flows. To be conservative, I've scaled the equity risk premium by a factor of 1.3 to reflect the current increased volatility in the market. There is no theory backing this 1.3, but I try to adopt a risk-averse approach in today's environment. With that said, I get an implied upside of 36%.

Key Risks

The current market sell-off amid rising tariffs globally and recessionary fears in the US market created a high-volatility environment. Given that Bilibili's earnings are currently in negative to low territory, it can be extra sensitive to sentiments. In addition, as a company operating in China, the implications of domestic macro-impact are dependent on the country's commitment to demand and consumption boost in lieu of a 5% GDP growth target for 2025.

For more company-specific risks, Bilibili's substantial improvement in earnings recently is subject to strong performance in advertising and gaming. For gaming, the popularity and trend of games shift through time, and that should be monitored on a quarterly basis. Advertising should also be closely watched as management only recently suggested that the improvement is due to better technological competencies, we should see down the road how persistent it really is.

Conclusion

Overall, I am bullish on BILI considering its operating strengths and its current valuation. The main downside to consider is the current sell-off environment for global equities that has yet to bottom with elevated volatilities. Meanwhile, earnings expectations are high but reachable, while revenue growth expectations are mild. Management is ambitious on gross margin increases, and that guidance would likely implicate significant outperformance of the current earnings expectations, which on the other hand, supports a relatively gradual margin expansion. The company has a proven track record in cost-cutting since 2022 with the more aggressive earnings driver being growth in advertising and gaming revenue where the company has shown significant improvement in capabilities. With that said, BILI's price target using a conservative residual income valuation gives a target share price of $21.2 or an upside of 28%.
